w:r.w:t: [Provide main workflows (business processes) using the swim-lane diagram(s) like sample below]
Review the quality of the syllabus and documental
Design or update training program content
Use syllabus and documental, upload new document
Guest enters a keyword to search for a curriculum
Display the list of curricula after searching
View detailed information of a specific curriculum
View Program Learning Outcomes of a curriculum
Guest enters a keyword to search for a subject
Display the list of subjects after searching
View detailed information of a specific subject
Download learning materials for a subject
Guest logs in to become a privileged user
Submit the completed curriculum for review
Upload learning materials to the system
Link learning materials to a specific topic
Upload curriculum data using an Excel file
Manage the mapping between CLOs and PLOs
Reviewer views the list of pending curricula
Provide a reason when rejecting a curriculum
Manage all curricula in the system
[Provide functionality overview of software system: screen flow, screen descriptions, system user roles, screen authorization, non-screen functions, ERD]
[This part shows the system screens and the relationship among screens. You can draw the Screens Flow for the system in the form of diagram as below.]
Allows users to log into the system using username and password
Main dashboard that provides navigation to all modules based on user role
Displays the list of training programs/curriculums
Shows detailed curriculum information including subjects and mappings
Displays recommended learning sequence and study roadmap
Displays mapping between PO, PLO, CLO, and subjects
Shows detailed syllabus information and attached documents
Allows lecturers/admins to upload syllabus or training documents
Allows authorized users to download training materials
Allows admin to manage user accounts
Allows admin to assign and manage user roles and permissions
Create, update, and maintain curriculum structures
Supports designing and structuring new academic programs
Allows curriculum structure planning and subject arrangement
Design subject structure, learning outcomes, and content
Design syllabus template and academic structure
Displays curriculums waiting for review or approval
Displays curriculum details for reviewer evaluation
Rejects curriculum or syllabus submissions with feedback

[Provide the system roles authorization to the system features (down to screens, and event to the screen activities if applicable) in the table form as below – replace Role-Name1, Role-Name2,… with your specific system user role names]
[Provide the descriptions for the non-screen system functions, i.e batch/cron job, service, API, etc.]
Verify username/password and generate user session/token
Check user permissions before accessing system functions
using the Crow-Foot notation and the entity descriptions as below]
Phân chia lại rõ ràng PK FK
Stores information of all system users including Admin, Designer, Reviewer, Lecturer, and Student.
Stores user role definitions and access permissions within the system.
Stores academic program information such as Software Engineering, Artificial Intelligence, etc.
Stores curriculum information belonging to a specific academic program.
Stores information about subjects/courses included in curricula.
Manages the relationship between curricula and subjects, including semester allocation.
Stores prerequisite relationships between subjects for learning path management.
Stores detailed syllabus specifications for a subject, including version and status information.
Stores weekly learning sessions/topics belonging to a syllabus.
Stores curriculum-related documents and uploaded learning materials.
Stores curriculum review, approval, and rejection records from reviewers.
Stores Program Objectives that define the educational goals of a curriculum.
Stores Program Learning Outcomes representing the expected competencies of graduates.
Stores Course Learning Outcomes representing subject-level learning achievements.
Stores mappings between Program Objectives and Program Learning Outcomes.
Stores mappings between Course Learning Outcomes and Program Learning Outcomes.
[Provide specifications for the use cases (UCs) those are covered in the system. The UCs are grouped by the system features and even sub features.
You just need to provide UC specifications for complex UCs involving in the main workflows (business processes)
. Other UCs (i.e CRUD or data-viewing UCs) are simple, and you just need to refer the descriptions in the Functional Requirement (part 3) below)]
Provide the functional description for the use cases using the template/guides below
User clicks Login button or accesses authenticated feature
Users log into the system to access functionalities based on their roles and permissions.
PRE-1. User accounts already exists in the system.
POST-1. User successfully accesses the system. POST-2. User activity is stored into Login History.
System determines user role and permissions.
System redirects user to Dashboard page.
System redirects to Google Authentication page.
System logs user into FLM system.
Return to step 5 of Normal Flow.

Give each use case a unique integer sequence number identifier. State a concise name for the use case that indicates the value the use case would provide to some user. Begin with an action verb, followed by an object.
Enter the name of the person who initially wrote this use case and the date it was written.

An actor is a person or other entity external to the software system being specified who interacts with the system and performs use cases to accomplish tasks. Different actors often correspond to different user classes, or roles, identified from the customer community that will use the product. Name the primary actor that will be initiating this use case and any other secondary actors who will participate in completing execution of the use case.

Identify the business event, system event, or user action that initiates the use case. This trigger alerts the system that it should begin testing the preconditions for the use case so it can judge whether to proceed with execution.
Provide a brief description of the reason for and outcome of this use case, or a high-level description of the sequence of actions and the outcome of executing the use case.

List any activities that must take place, or any conditions that must be true, before the use case can be started. The system must be able to test each precondition. Number each precondition. Example: PRE-1: User’s identity has been authenticated.

Describe the state of the system at the successful conclusion of the use case execution. Label each postcondition in the form POST-X, where X is a sequence number. Example: POST-1: Price of item in the database has been updated with the new value.

Provide a description of the user actions and corresponding system responses that will take place during execution of the use case under normal, expected conditions. This dialog sequence will ultimately lead to accomplishing the goal stated in the use case name and description. Show a numbered list of actions performed by the actor, alternating with responses provided by the system. The normal flow is numbered “X.0”, where “X” is the Use Case ID.

Document other successful usage scenarios that can take place within this use case. State the alternative flow, and describe any differences in the sequence of steps that take place. Number each alternative flow in the form “X.Y”, where “X” is the Use Case ID and Y is a sequence number for the alternative flow. For example, “5.3” would indicate the third alternative flow for use case number 5. Indicate where each alternative flow would branch off from the normal flow, and if pertinent, where it would rejoin the normal flow.

Describe any anticipated error conditions that could occur during execution of the use case and how the system is to respond to those conditions. Number each alternative flow in the form “X.Y.EZ”, where “X” is the Use Case ID, Y indicates the normal (0) or alternative (>0) flow during which this exception could take place, “E” indicates an exception, and “Z” is a sequence number for the exceptions. For example “5.0.E2” would indicate the second exception for the normal flow for use case number 5. Indicate where in the normal (or an alternative) flow each exception could occur.
Indicate the relative priority of implementing the functionality required to allow this use case to be executed. Use the same priority scheme as that used for the functional requirements.
Estimate the number of times this use case will be performed per some appropriate unit of time. This gives an early indicator of throughput, concurrent usage loads, and transaction capacity.
List any business rules that influence this use case. Don’t include the business rule text here, just its identifier so the reader can find it in another repository when needed.

Identify any additional requirements, such as quality attributes, for the use case that may need to be addressed during design or implementation. Also list any associated functional requirements that aren’t a direct part of the use case flows but which a developer needs to know about. Describe what should happen if the use case execution fails for some unanticipated or systemic reason (e.g., loss of network connectivity, timeout). If the use case results in a durable state change in a database or the outside world, state whether the change is rolled back, completed correctly, partially completed with a known state, or left in an undetermined state as a result of the exception.
List any assumptions that were made regarding this use case or how it might execute.
Provide the business rules those are applied only to the use case
User passwords must be encrypted before storing into database
Users can only access features permitted to their roles
Guest, Student, Lecturer, Reviewer, Designer, Admin
User inputs keyword into subject search field
Users search for subjects by subject code, subject name, semester, or department.
PRE-1. Subject data exists in the system database.
POST-1. System displays matching subject list.
User inputs keyword into search field.
Return to step 5 of Normal Flow.
Only approved and active subjects are visible to Guest and Student
Users view personal account information and role details.
POST-1. System displays user profile successfully.
System retrieves selected user profile information.
Guest enters a keyword to search for a curriculum. The system queries the database and displays relevant active curriculums.
User accesses the system and navigates to the Curriculum search bar.
PRE-1. The system database contains approved and active curriculum records.
POST-1. The system displays a list of curriculums matching the search criteria (triggers UC02_View List Curriculum ).
User inputs a keyword (e.g., Curriculum Name, Code) into the search field.
User clicks the "Search" button or presses Enter.
User clicks the "Search" button or presses Enter.
System displays the search results in a list format (Includes UC02).
User selects specific filters (e.g., Department, Academic Year) before clicking Search.
System appends filter conditions to the database query.
Return to step 4 of Normal Flow.
1. System finds no matching records in the database.
2. System displays a message: "No curriculum found matching your search criteria."
3. System prompts the user to try different keywords.
Very High (Multiple times per session by all user roles).
Guests and Students can only search and view curriculums that have been officially "Approved" and marked as "Public".
View detailed information of a specific curriculum, including its subjects and Program Learning Outcomes (PLO).
User clicks on a specific curriculum record from the search results list.
PRE-1. The user has executed UC02_View List Curriculum.
POST-1. The system displays the detailed view of the selected curriculum
User selects a curriculum from the list.
System displays the curriculum overview (Name, Code, Description).
User can choose to view Program Learning Outcomes (Includes UC04_View PLO).
User can view the list of subjects belonging to this curriculum (Includes UC06_View List Subject ).
COS informs Patron that payroll deduction is established.
1. System fails to retrieve the record (e.g., it was just deleted or deactivated by an Admin).
2. System displays an error: "This curriculum is no longer available."
3. System redirects the user back to the search page.
Download learning materials for a subject. The system verifies permissions and logs the download action.
User clicks the "Download" icon next to a learning material within the Subject Details screen.
PRE-1. User is authenticated and logged into the system.
PRE-2. User is on the Subject Detail screen viewing available materials (UC07).
POST-1. The requested file is successfully downloaded to the user's local device.
POST-2. System records the transaction in
1. User clicks the download button for a specific material.
2. System invokes Role Permission Validation to check if the user is authorized to download materials for this subject.
3. System retrieves the file from the storage repository.
4. System initiates the file transfer to the user's browser.
5. System inserts a new record into the
1. System cannot locate the physical file in the storage path.
2. System displays an error message: "File is missing or corrupted. Please contact the administrator."
1. System determines the user lacks necessary permissions during step 2.
2. System denies the request and displays "Access Denied."
Every successful download must be logged in the
table for reporting and tracking usage statistics.
The designer submits a curriculum for review. This shifts the curriculum from "Draft" to "Pending Review" status, making it visible to Reviewers.
The designer completes editing a draft and clicks "Submit for Review".
1. The designer opens a saved curriculum draft.
3. System checks for completeness of all mandatory fields.
4. The system initiates the file transfer to the user's browser.
5. System alerts the Reviewer via Email Gateway.
Reviewer rejects a curriculum. The Reviewer must provide a reason, and the curriculum is sent back to the Designer for revision.
The reviewer determines the curriculum does not meet standards and clicks "Reject".
PRE-1. The curriculum is in "Pending Review" status.
POST-1. Status is changed to "Rejected" or "Draft".
POST-2. The Designer receives a rejection notification with the reason.
3. System prompts for a mandatory rejection reason (Includes UC18_Provide Rejection Reason ).
4. The reviewer inputs the reason and confirms.
6. The system sends the rejection feedback to the Designer via Email Gateway.
This screen allows the User to:
Enter Email: enter an email address to access the system.
Enter Password: enter a password for authentication.
Use Remember Account Option: choose whether the system remembers the login account on the current device.
Login to the System: access the system after entering valid credentials.
On the screen, s/he can also:
Recover Password: click Forgot Password? to open the password recovery screen.
Save Login Information: select Remember account to save login information for future logins.
Save the login account for future use.
This screen allows the User to:
Enter Email: enter the registered email address.
Request Verification Code: send a verification code to the entered email address.
Start Password Recovery: begin the password reset process.
On the screen, s/he can also:
Return to Login Screen: click Back to Login to return to the login page.
Receive Verification Code: click Send Verification Code to receive a verification code via email.
Tells the user to enter an email address to receive a verification code.
Send a verification code to the entered email address.
This screen allows the Student to:
-View Curriculum List: view all available training programs
-Search Curriculum: enter keyword(s) to search by code, name, or description
-View Curriculum Details: read detailed information directly from the list
On the screen, Student can also:
-Quickly compare curriculums based on credits and decision date
Data type: string, max length 100 characters; used to search curriculum by name
This screen allows the Student to:
-View Syllabus List: view all available syllabuses
-Search Syllabus: enter keyword(s) to search by ID, subject code, or subject name
-View Syllabus Information: read syllabus details such as subject, status, and decision number
On the screen, Student can also:
Data type: string, max length 100 characters; used to search curriculum by name
This screen allows the Student to:
-Search Subject: enter subject name to view its learning path
-View Learning Path: see prerequisite subjects required before studying the selected subject
-View Syllabus Information: read syllabus details such as name and decision date
On the screen, Student can also:
-Understand learning sequence before taking a subject
Data type: string, max length 100 characters; used to search curriculum by name
All subjects need to learn before
Data type: list of strings (prerequisite subjects)
This screen allows the Administrator to:
Filter and search users: The administrator can narrow down the displayed list by using the "List by" filter or the Search bar.
View user details: Allows the administrator to click the "View detail" button to see the profile and in-depth information of a specific user.
Send notifications: Allows the administrator to send system notifications directly to a user via the "Notification" button.
The display name or full name of the user in the system (e.g., Jame, Joe, Adam).
The unique identifier of the user, a string starting with a
The date and time the user account was created in the system.
An action button, with different background colors (solid green or white with a blue outline) depending on the context, used to open the detailed information page.
The current operational status of the account:
An additional action button providing the
option to interact or send direct alerts to the user.
This screen allows the Administrator to:
Switch between the "Profile", "Transactions", and "Notification" tabs to view different categories of user information.
Review detailed personal and contact information of the selected user.
Modify the operational status of the user's account using the dropdown menu (e.g., changing from Active to Banned).
Apply and update any modifications made to the user's status or profile by clicking the green "Save" button.
Navigation tabs to switch between the user's profile details, transaction history, and notification logs.
Data type: String. Displays the full name of the user (e.g., Charlie Jame).
Data type: Numeric string. Displays the unique identifier associated with the user (e.g., 123456789).
Data type: String (Email format). Displays the registered email address of the user.
Data type: Numeric string. Displays the contact phone number of the user.
This screen allows the Designer to:
Import curriculum information from an Excel file.
Data type: string, max length of 50 characters, unique value, required
Data type: string, max length of 500 characters, required
Data type: string, max length of 500 characters
Data type: rich text, unlimited length
Data type: string, max length of 100 characters
Data type: positive integer, range from 1 to 300
Data type: .xlsx file, maximum size 20 MB
This tab allows the Academic Designer to:
view a comprehensive data table of all subjects currently registered in the curriculum layout.
dynamically filter the active table rows by typing keywords corresponding to specific subject codes or names.
On this tab, s/he can also:
click the designated creation button to open a form or modal for appending a new subject to the database.
select the modification control icon for any specific row to update its properties.
select the deletion control icon for any specific row to purge that record from the curriculum framework.
Input field. Allows the user to type in keywords to filter the subject grid dynamically by Subject Code or Subject Name.
Action button. Click to navigate to the input screen or toggle the modal for adding a new subject into the curriculum.
Column header & Data cell. Displays the distinct alphanumeric code assigned to each individual course (e.g., CS101, CS201).
Column header & Data cell. Displays the official educational title or descriptor of the course (e.g., Introduction to Programming).
Column header & Data cell. Displays the numeric academic credit value weighted for the corresponding subject row.
Column header & Data cell. Identifies the numerical sequence of the academic semester recommended for students to take the subject.
Column containing control elements for row operations:
Triggers the editing mechanism for the selected subject record.
Triggers a removal validation prompt to permanently drop the subject row.
This tab allows the Academic Designer to:
View PO List: view the list of defined Program Objectives including PO Code and their corresponding descriptions.
On this tab, s/he can also:
Add New PO: click the "+ Add PO" button to open the form or modal for creating a new Program Objective.
Edit PO: click the Edit icon (pencil) in the Actions column to update an existing PO's information or description.
Delete PO: click the Delete icon (trash bin) in the Actions column to remove a PO from the curriculum framework.
Action button. Click to navigate to the screen or toggle the modal for adding a new Program Objective.
Column header & Data cell. Displays the unique code identifier for each Program Objective (e.g., PO1, PO2).
Column header & Data cell. Displays the specific educational objective statement or target defined for the corresponding PO code.
Column containing control elements for row operations:
Opens the modification form/modal for the selected PO row.
Triggers a confirmation prompt to permanently delete the selected PO record.
This tab allows the Academic Designer to:
View PLO List: view the list of defined Program Learning Outcomes including PLO Code, their core descriptions, and the corresponding mapped Program Objectives (PO).
On this tab, s/he can also:
Add New PLO: click the "+ Add PLO" button to open the form or modal for creating a new Program Learning Outcome.
Edit PLO: click the Edit icon (pencil) in the Actions column to update an existing PLO's details or structural mapping.
Delete PLO: click the Delete icon (trash bin) in the Actions column to remove a PLO record from the curriculum layout.
Action button. Click to navigate to the entry screen or toggle the configuration modal for adding a new Program Learning Outcome.
Column header & Data cell. Displays the distinct alphanumeric code assigned to each Program Learning Outcome (e.g., PLO1, PLO2).
Column header & Data cell. Displays the descriptive competency statement or target knowledge standard expected for the corresponding PLO row.
Column header & Data cell. Displays the linked Program Objective codes associated with the current outcome row (e.g., PO1, PO3).
Column containing operational elements for individual row execution:
Opens the modification dashboard for the selected PLO data entry.
Triggers a removal validation prompt to securely delete the selected PLO profile.
This tab allows the Academic Designer to:
View CLO List: view the data table listing all defined Course Learning Outcomes along with their descriptions and mapped Program Learning Outcomes (PLO).
On this tab, s/he can also:
Add New CLO: click the "+ Add CLO" button to open the form or modal for creating a new Course Learning Outcome.
Edit CLO: click the Edit icon (pencil) in the Actions column to update an existing CLO's details or relational mapping.
Delete CLO: click the Delete icon (trash bin) in the Actions column to remove a CLO record from the system.
Action button. Click to navigate to the data entry layout or trigger the configuration modal for adding a new Course Learning Outcome.
Column header & Data cell. Displays the distinct alphanumeric code assigned to each Course Learning Outcome (e.g., CLO1, CLO2).
Column header & Data cell. Displays the specific skill, knowledge, or behavioral statement expected for students to achieve in the corresponding CLO row.
Column header & Data cell. Displays the parent Program Learning Outcome codes linked to the current outcome row (e.g., PLO1, PLO2).
Column containing management icons for row execution:
Toggles the editing interface for the selected CLO row record.
Triggers a removal validation prompt to permanently drop the selected CLO profile from the curriculum.
This tab allows the Academic Designer to:
View PLO / PO Matrix: view an interactive correlation grid that shows the current mappings between Program Learning Outcomes (PLO) and Program Objectives (PO).
Configure Matrix Relationships: select or deselect specific intersections to establish or modify the alignment structure between academic objectives and learning outcomes.
Grid header cell. Indicates the intersecting axis orientation where rows represent Program Learning Outcomes and columns represent Program Objectives.
Column headers. Represent the individual Program Objectives defined within the curriculum structure.
Row headers. Represent the individual Program Learning Outcomes defined within the curriculum structure.
Indicates an active mapping link between the corresponding PLO row and PO column.
Indicates no existing relationship between the corresponding PLO and PO.
This tab allows the Academic Designer to:
drag and drop files or browse the local device to upload official supporting documents related to the curriculum framework.
track a comprehensive data grid displaying successfully uploaded materials along with their formatting and storage metadata.
On this tab, s/he can also:
click the eye icon to quickly review the contents of an attached file directly within the application viewer framework.
click the trash bin icon to remove an outdated or incorrect attachment from the current curriculum workspace.
Drag & Drop Area (Upload PDF or DOCX files)
Interactive dashed boundary zone. Allows users to directly drag and drop authorized file formats (.pdf or .docx) to trigger an automated system upload.
Action button. Click to launch the local operating system's File Explorer dialog to browse and select files for upload.
Column header & Data field. Displays the complete title of the uploaded file alongside its respective format-identifying system icon.
Column header & Data field. Displays the specific file extension format (e.g., PDF, DOCX).
Column header & Data field. Displays the actual data footprint or file capacity stored on the server (e.g., 2.4 MB, 1.2 MB).
Column header & Data field. Displays the timestamp markers (YYYY-MM-DD) indicating when the file record was committed to the application.
Column containing target operational controls for row executions:
Launches an online document preview modal window for quick document auditing.
Triggers a modal validation prompt to securely and permanently detach the selected file.
This screen allows the Reviewer to:
view all submitted curricula/documents that require review.
open the curriculum/document link to review its content.
view the current activation status and approval status of each curriculum.
filter review records by activation status or approval status.
enter keyword(s) to search review records by curriculum name or lecturer name.
On the screen, s/he can also:
change the active status of a selected curriculum.
approve a curriculum after completing the review process.
click the curriculum link to review supporting documents and curriculum details.
Shows the number of each review item.
Shows the curriculum link. Click the link to view the curriculum details.
Shows the name of the lecturer who created or submitted the curriculum.
Shows whether the curriculum is active or not. Reviewer can check or uncheck this box to change the status.
Shows whether the curriculum has been approved or not. Reviewer can check this box to approve the curriculum.
This screen allows the Reviewer to:
View Program Information: view curriculum information such as curriculum code, name, decision number, and description.
View Program Learning Outcomes (PLOs): view all PLOs of the curriculum.
View Subject List: view all subjects included in the curriculum.
View Review Comments: view or enter comments related to the review.
Review Curriculum Content: check curriculum information before making a review decision.
On the screen, s/he can also:
Approve Curriculum: approve the curriculum by clicking the Approve button.
Reject Curriculum: reject the curriculum by clicking the Reject button.
Add Comments: enter comments or suggestions in the comment box.
View Program Outcomes: click the View PO button to open the Program Outcomes screen.
View Curriculum Combos: click the View Combo button to open the Combo Details screen.
View Elective Subjects: click the View Elective button to open the Elective Subjects screen.
Return to Review List: click Back Review List to return to the previous screen.
Return to the review list screen.
Shows all Program Learning Outcomes of the curriculum.
Shows all subjects in the curriculum.
This screen allows the Teacher to:
Upload File: Drag and drop an Excel file directly into the designated area or click the "Browse Files" button to select a file from local storage.
Define Document Information: Provide metadata to categorize the uploaded file, including subject code, class code, title, document type, and semester.
Add Description: Use the rich text editor to add notes, instructions, or detailed descriptions for the file.
Save Data: Click the save button to complete the upload process and record the information into the system database.
The file upload area. Users can drag and drop a file (
Drag and drop your Excel file here
) or click the Browse Files button to browse and select an Excel file from their computer.
Text input. Used to enter the subject code corresponding to the uploaded data (Placeholder:
Text input. Used to enter the corresponding class code (Placeholder:
Text input. Used to specify a title or name for the uploaded document or dataset (Placeholder:
Dropdown list. Allows categorizing the file. Available options include:
Dropdown list. Allows selecting the semester applicable to the data. Available options include:
Rich text editor area. Provides a basic formatting toolbar (Bold, Italic, Underline, Alignment, List, Insert Link). Used to enter additional notes or detailed descriptions for the file (Placeholder:
Enter notes or description for this file...
Green action button. Used to trigger the process of saving all form information and the uploaded file into the system.
This section describes how the FLM system interacts with users and external systems.
The system shall provide a web-based graphical user interface accessible through modern web browsers.
The user interface shall support responsive design for desktop, tablet, and mobile devices.
The system shall provide role-based dashboards for:
The interface shall support the English language.
Navigation menus shall be displayed according to the user's assigned role.
The system shall provide search, filtering, sorting, and pagination for all major modules.
The FLM system shall be easy to learn and operate for users with different roles.
A new student shall be able to search and view learning materials within 5 minutes without training.
A lecturer shall be able to upload learning materials within 10 minutes after first login.
A reviewer shall be able to review and approve a syllabus within 15 minutes after training.
All pages shall follow a consistent navigation structure.
Buttons, forms, and data tables shall maintain consistent layouts across modules.
The system shall support keyboard navigation.
The system shall provide readable font sizes.
The system shall comply with WCAG 2.1 Level AA guidelines where feasible.
Mandatory fields shall be clearly marked.
Validation messages shall be displayed before submission.
Users shall receive meaningful error messages when operations fail.
At least 90% of surveyed users shall successfully complete common tasks without assistance.
The system shall provide acceptable response times under normal operating conditions.
Database connection pool utilization shall remain below 75%.
The system shall provide stable and dependable operation.
System availability shall be at least 99.5%.
No single transaction shall result in data corruption.
All critical operations shall use database transactions.
Automatic recovery shall occur after unexpected failures.
The system shall protect academic data and user information.
Users shall authenticate through FPT authentication services.
Passwords shall never be stored in plain text.
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) shall be implemented.
Permissions shall be enforced at both UI and API levels.
Sensitive information shall be encrypted in transit.
Audit logs shall record all critical modifications.
Session timeout shall occur after 30 minutes of inactivity.
Multiple failed login attempts shall trigger temporary account lockout.
The system shall be easy to modify and maintain.
The system shall follow a layered architecture.
Business logic shall be separated from presentation logic.
Database schema shall satisfy Third Normal Form (3NF).
Source code shall follow coding standards and naming conventions.
New modules shall be integrated without affecting existing modules.
The system shall support future growth.
The system shall support horizontal application scaling.
Database performance shall remain acceptable when data volume doubles.
Additional campuses and academic programs shall be added without schema redesign.
The system shall remain accessible during academic operations.
System uptime shall be at least 99.5%.
Planned maintenance shall be announced at least 24 hours in advance.
Critical services shall be monitored continuously.
The system shall maintain complete audit trails.
All create, update, delete operations shall be logged.
Workflow approvals and rejections shall be traceable.
Login history shall be retained for at least 12 months.
Audit logs shall not be editable by normal users.
The system shall ensure consistency and correctness of data.
Foreign key constraints shall be enforced.
Duplicate subject codes shall not be allowed.
Duplicate curriculum codes shall not be allowed.
Version histories shall be preserved for syllabus modifications.
Transaction rollback shall occur upon failure.
